behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Recursive watch behavior commonly depends on real filesystem watcher semantics and OS event delivery. An in-process mock can falsely pass even when subdirectory events are never emitted in production.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon against a temporary root directory on disk. Create a nested subdirectory (for example `root/a/b`) and write a new input file there. Assert the transformed output appears in the output directory within timeout and that logs/metrics show exactly one processing attempt for that nested path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This can be reproduced deterministically at a single object/function boundary (dedup cache/window check) with direct inputs and controlled time. No OS or network behavior is required to catch core logic failures.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the dedup component with a short window and a fake/controllable clock. Submit path `p` at `t0` (accepted), submit `p` again at `t0+delta` where `delta < window` (rejected), then at `t0+window+epsilon` (accepted). Also verify path normalization expectation used by dedup (exact key behavior) is explicit.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is real OS watcher behavior (duplicate create/change/close events). Mocking event streams can miss backend-specific duplication patterns that happen in production.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with real watcher on a temp directory. Perform a write pattern known to emit multiple events (create + append + fsync/close or atomic write via temp+rename). Capture processing count for that file via output artifacts and/or instrumentation, and assert exactly one completed transformation.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Directory deletion/recreation resilience depends on OS file notification handles and watcher re-subscription behavior. In-process mocks often hide lost-watch bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching temp dir `root`. Verify baseline by processing one file. Delete `root` while daemon stays running, recreate `root`, then place a new file in recreated directory. Assert daemon processes the new file without restart and does not require manual reconfiguration.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on real file timestamp metadata and event timing/races. Mocks can impose idealized ordering and miss platform timestamp granularity or event interleaving issues.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon on a real temp filesystem. Create several files nearly simultaneously with controlled creation times (or staged writes to produce distinct timestamps). Record processing order via daemon instrumentation/output sequence and assert it matches ascending creation timestamp, including deterministic tie-break behavior if timestamps collide.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This requires real concurrent file I/O and watcher timing. Mocking writer completion can hide partial-read races that only appear with real OS buffering/flush behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a temp input dir. Open a large file for streaming write and keep descriptor open while writing chunks slowly. Confirm daemon does not process during the in-progress write (no output or failed partial transform). Close the file, then assert processing occurs exactly once and output reflects full final content.
